article.title: Allen Control Systems Raises $200 Million Series B at $2.2 Billion Valuation to Scale Its Bullfrog Autonomous Counter-Drone Gun
article.summary: Counter-drone startup Allen Control Systems closed a $200 million Series B at a $2.2 billion valuation to scale Bullfrog, an autonomous weapon station already deployed with the U.S. Army and Navy.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Allen Control Systems (ACS), an Austin, Texas startup building autonomous robotic gun mounts to shoot down drones, has raised a $200 million Series B at a $2.2 billion post-money valuation, according to [The Robot Report](https://www.therobotreport.com/allen-control-systems-raises-200m-for-autonomous-weapons-stations/). The company says the capital will scale manufacturing and accelerate deployment of Bullfrog, its flagship autonomous weapon station, which is already fielded with the U.S. Army and U.S. Navy.

The round was led by Smash Capital, with participation from Craft Ventures, Rally Ventures, Inspired Capital, and others, as reported by [The Robot Report](https://www.therobotreport.com/allen-control-systems-raises-200m-for-autonomous-weapons-stations/).

## What We Know

Bullfrog is described by [The Robot Report](https://www.therobotreport.com/allen-control-systems-raises-200m-for-autonomous-weapons-stations/) as "a lightweight, low-power autonomous weapon station that combines AI, computer vision, and precision robotics." The system is designed to detect, identify, and neutralize enemy unmanned systems and is capable of engaging Group 1 through Group 3 unmanned aircraft, operating in both autonomous and semi-autonomous modes.

Rather than relying on costly missile interceptors, Bullfrog integrates with existing firearms. According to [Defense Daily](https://www.defensedaily.com/bullfrog-counter-drone-developer-allen-control-systems-raises-200-million/business-financial/), ACS said it raised the money "to scale production of its Bullfrog autonomous weapon station that can be integrated with different weapons—including the M240 machine gun—to defeat close-in drone threats." The cost case is central to the company's pitch. "Bullfrog provides governments with a scalable and affordable solution that leverages our nearly unlimited supply of bullets all over the world, compared to scarce and expensive missiles and interceptors," said Mike Wior, ACS co-founder and CEO, as quoted by [The Robot Report](https://www.therobotreport.com/allen-control-systems-raises-200m-for-autonomous-weapons-stations/).

The system is no longer a prototype. ACS has received contracts through Joint Interagency Task Force 401, and Bullfrog is deployed with the U.S. Army and U.S. Navy, according to [The Robot Report](https://www.therobotreport.com/allen-control-systems-raises-200m-for-autonomous-weapons-stations/). The same report states that "Bullfrog demonstrated a 100% success rate during Technology Readiness Experiment 2026 (T-REX 26-1)."

The funding comes against a backdrop of intensifying demand for systems that can counter cheap, mass-produced drones. "Drone threats are growing faster than traditional air defense systems can meet them," Wior said, per [The Robot Report](https://www.therobotreport.com/allen-control-systems-raises-200m-for-autonomous-weapons-stations/). Co-founder and President Steve Simoni added that "Global demand for remote weapon stations has reached unprecedented levels as governments recognize kinetic defeat as the most reliable solution against low-cost weaponized drones," according to [The Robot Report](https://www.therobotreport.com/allen-control-systems-raises-200m-for-autonomous-weapons-stations/).

Lead investor Smash Capital echoed that framing. "ACS has demonstrated a prescient understanding of the trajectory of modern warfare," said Paul Szurek, a partner at the firm, as reported by [The Robot Report](https://www.therobotreport.com/allen-control-systems-raises-200m-for-autonomous-weapons-stations/).

## What We Don't Know

The cited reporting does not disclose ACS's revenue, the size or value of its government contracts, or how many Bullfrog units have been delivered to date. The number of additional product lines the company plans to develop with the new capital, and the timeline for scaling manufacturing, were not specified. The conditions and scope of the T-REX 26-1 evaluation behind the reported 100 percent success rate were also not detailed in the fetched coverage.

## Analysis

The raise places ACS among a growing cohort of defense-technology companies betting that the next phase of air defense will be fought not with expensive interceptors but with autonomous robotics bolted onto conventional firearms. By framing Bullfrog as a way to make standard weapons such as the M240 effective against drones, ACS is targeting a cost asymmetry that has bedeviled militaries facing swarms of inexpensive unmanned aircraft. The $2.2 billion valuation reported by [The Robot Report](https://www.therobotreport.com/allen-control-systems-raises-200m-for-autonomous-weapons-stations/) reflects investor appetite for counter-drone systems that have moved past demonstration and into fielded use.
